concepts.description: High vs low stance set by foot width; the stability/mobility trade-off.
Guard and fist placement: correct height and spacing of the hands.
Lead foot angled slightly inward for balance and punching power.
Weight distribution and staying balanced in the stance.
Keeping shoulders and pelvis in one vertical plane.
Compact torso, elbows in; sensing and protecting the body's boundaries.
Switching orthodox/southpaw stance safely — never switch without a punch.
Fighting as, or against, a southpaw.
The basic categories of footwork and how to step.
The sidestep, under-arm and over-arm variants.
Sliding / gliding steps.
Foot speed and coordination (ladder-type work).
Cutting angles and changing direction to get off the line.
Using footwork (with extended arms) to stay out of range and punch on the move.
What the pendulum (shuttle) step is and why/when to use it.
'Locking the ankle' — the calf/ankle spring that drives the bounce.
The weight-transfer timing and rhythm of the pendulum.
Sustaining the pendulum without tiring; when NOT to bounce all round.
The transverse (sideways) pendulum variant.
Throwing punches out of the pendulum.
Slipping and head-movement while in the pendulum.
The peekaboo-style pendulum (Tyson).
Whole-body, ground-up single smooth punch (the kinetic chain).
Splitting the body upper/lower and left/right for balance and power.
Relaxed delivery, snap and accentuation; the 'preparatory stop'.
Punching through the target — the punch has no early ending.
No telegraph: no wind-up, no pre-rotation of the fist, no jerking the hand back.
Clenching the fist at the moment of impact (timing).
Whether and when to rotate (pronate) the fist.
Landing on the correct knuckles; the fist's contact position.
Knuckle and hand protection; why the skin rubs; wrist lock.
The jab / straight lead: fundamentals and common mistakes.
Variants of the jab (e.g. the underhand jab).
The double jab.
Using the jab to get inside, stall, or lead the entry.
The straight rear hand (cross): reach and the collarbone line.
The overhand right, including hands-down delivery (GGG style).
Fundamental hook technique — the key detail that makes it correct.
Vertical vs horizontal hook: when each is blocked or goes around.
Keeping the chin covered while hooking.
The leaping / lunging hook to close distance.
Using the hook to go around or obviate the opponent's guard.
Fundamental uppercut technique and biomechanics.
The uppercut to the body.
The uppercut at clinch / very-close range.
The shot to the liver.
Attacking the body generally; multi-shot body series and levels.
The one-two: hand exchange and its three-movement structure (incl. the defensive third).
Linking punches: hand return not jerk, back/leg coordination, gravity transitions.
The one-two-three combination and why three is the optimal series.
Varying rhythm/tempo to deceive — and the rule not to change tempo mid-combination.
Combinations engineered to pry open the guard (incl. the liver guard).
Single accented shots vs series/flurries — the fundamental difference.
Feints to deceive and set up an entry.
Provoking / drawing the opponent's lead in order to counter.
Countering fundamentals; offense as defense; what to do after you miss.
Intercepting / stop-hitting the attack as it starts.
Countering immediately after a slip.
Using the uppercut as the counter (incl. underhand counters).
Drawing the opponent, then countering with the straight right.
Seizing the initiative and initiating rather than waiting.
Overcoming fear — of throwing, of exchanges, of being countered.
Soviet-style slip technique — taking the head off the line of attack.
The component movements of slipping and ducking (legs and weight).
Classical trunk / bodywork (weave, roll) for defense and infighting.
Getting off the line of fire after the slip — one small step to safety.
The closed guard.
Covering head and torso; blocking and parrying with the arms.
The shoulder-roll defense.
Using the shoulder to defend hooks to the chin.
Not blinking or flinching; getting used to being hit.
Defending against hooks.
Defending against the overhand.
Countering the American hanging jab.
Reading and defending an incoming uppercut.
The geometry of boxing: lines, angles and positioning.
Operating and attacking at long range; committing without fear of 'falling'.
Closing / shortening distance to get inside.
Closing distance covertly and deceptively (the secret entry).
Controlling range: keeping a taller foe at bay and solving reach problems.
Fighting a taller / longer opponent — getting inside and catching the counter.
Fighting a shorter opponent.
Handling an aggressive pressure / bulldozing opponent, and stalling momentum.
Style matchups (swarmer vs out-boxer).
Applying infighting pressure while keeping balance.
Close-range combination work.
Proper actions on and near the ropes.
Escaping the corner.
Endurance and stamina (rhythm change, pulse control).
General and punching strength via weights (dumbbells, barbell, 'coin principle').
Explosive / plyometric power.
Core stability and balance (BOSU, ball).
Pulling strength and the shoulder girdle (pull-ups).
Pressing / push-up power (push-up machines).
Jump-rope conditioning and coordination.
Running / roadwork for punch coordination.
Ankle and calf strengthening for the spring.
Speed-bag proficiency.
Double-end-bag timing.
Heavy-bag work and shot placement.
Band / stick / barbell / bottle / sledgehammer drills to groove mechanics.
Reaction and accuracy tools (laser pointer, tennis ball, Evnik, flying-away bag).
Warm-up and mobility to avoid injury (and protect the spine).
Post-workout stretching, relaxation and recovery.
Mental preparation, focus and meditation; tuning in for a fight.
Boxing philosophy: talent vs work, the key quality, age to start, technique-first.
Putting the skills together under live sparring conditions.
Pad / mitt-holding methodology (coach-facing).
Analysis and breakdown of professional fights and champions' styles.
Channel, course, club and camp promotion and greetings.
Equipment selection and care (gloves).
